name: Astaxanthin
oneliner: Carotenoid antioxidant with replicated elasticity and MED data.
headline: Astaxanthin

evidence.note: Modest reduction in MED at 4–6 mg/d after 9 weeks. Not a sunscreen replacement.
Replicated bioengineering improvement; visible benefit modest.
Some signal; not the headline most marketing implies.

forms.v: Sunscreen + topical vitamin C. Astaxanthin is an *adjunct* to photoprotection, not a stand-in.
Natural (Haematococcus pluvialis) preferred — synthetic exists but data thinner.
4–12 mg/d with food; 6–8 mg covers most published endpoints.
9–12 weeks before judging effect. Stop earlier and you've wasted the trial.

faq.a: No, and any marketing that implies so is misleading. Oral astaxanthin produces a modest reduction in UV-induced erythema after 6–9 weeks, on the order of 10–20% — useful as an adjunct, useless as a substitute.

Natural Haematococcus-derived astaxanthin has the bulk of the published in-vivo skin data. Synthetic astaxanthin (used in aquaculture) is structurally similar but its published clinical data is thinner. Spend the small premium for the natural form.
Yes — well-tolerated up to 12 mg/d in trials lasting 6 months. Mild stool-orange discoloration possible at high doses (it is a carotenoid).
Mild blood-thinning effect at high doses. If you are on warfarin or DOACs, mention to your doctor before starting.
